Final Project - Predicting PDAC with Circulating miRNA

2026-04-22

Preparing Data

Import Data

We use the normalized miRNA count matrix from GEO, apply a single log2 transform with a pseudocount of 1, and then merge it with the demographic metadata. Categorical variables are set to factors and the PDAC case and control variable is set to binary for modeling. This ensures that the data is in a suitable format for downstream analysis, including differential expression analysis and predictive modeling.

Like the authors of the original study, we remove three specific miRNA that could be contaminated by the blood withdrawal process: - MIMAT00001631 hsa-miR-451a - MIMAT0000069 hsa-miR-16-5p - MIMAT0015072 hsa-miR-320e

The side-by-side QQ plots below show how the global miRNA value distribution shifts after the import-time log2 transform. The non-linearity of the miRNA counts is evident both before and after log2 transformation. Linearity is non a requirement for the downstream models, but the log2 transformation helps stabilize variance.

The dataset is fully observed with no missing values, so we can proceed without imputation.

Create Training and Holdout Sets

To split the data while respecting matched-pairs, we performed the split on the matched set IDs, ensuring each pair is not split between sets.
Training and holdout set sizes
dataset rows
Training 2090
Holdout 524
Training and holdout class balance
dataset class n_samples
Training Controls 1045
Training PDAC case 1045
Holdout Controls 262
Holdout PDAC case 262

Perform Differential Expression Filtering

The limma filtering is based on a blocked linear model with duplicate-correlation adjustment for matched sets and is run once on the full training set. CC is the term of interest and Sex, Race, and Age are adjustment covariates. The top N miRNAs are selected based on the smallest limma p-values, with ties broken by the absolute moderated t-statistic.

Top 25 miRNAs Selected by Differential Expression

The top-ranked miRNAs table has 25 rows and 10 columns. The selected miRNAs vector has 25 miRNAs. These top 25 miRNAs remain the default candidate feature set for the forward-step, lasso, elastic-net, and LDA models in the subsequent cross-validation step. The random forest and decision tree models are instead given the full log2-transformed miRNA set without limma prefiltering.

Cross-Validation Loop

We perform a 10-fold cross-validation on the training set of paired samples, ensuring matched-samples are kept together in the same fold.

Within each fold: 1. We restrict the log2-transformed normalized count matrix to the top 25 limma-selected miRNAs for the forward-step, lasso, elastic-net, and LDA models, while the random forest and decision tree models receive the full log2-transformed miRNA set without limma prefiltering. 2. We then fit each model using its assigned miRNA input pool together with Sex, Race, and Age in the design. 3. The LDA and forward-step models additionally standardize only the selected miRNA log-count columns using training-fold means and standard deviations before fitting, and apply the same transformation to the corresponding validation data. 4. The random forest model additionally evaluates 15, 20, and 25 as candidate mtry values and keeps the fit with the lowest out-of-bag classification error.

Cross-validation AUC and accuracy by model
model n_predictions cv_auc cv_accuracy
lasso 2090 0.568 0.538
enet 2090 0.567 0.538
lda 2090 0.562 0.542
forward_step 2090 0.560 0.534
decision_tree 2090 0.517 0.512
random_forest 2090 0.508 0.505

Holdout Evaluation

Evaluate the final fold, using the calibrated models from the CV step, on the holdout set. We report AUC and accuracy for each model on the holdout set, as well as the number of features selected in each final model and the size of each model’s input miRNA pool.

decision_tree 0.492 0.490

Random Forest Out-of-Bag Error by Tree Count

For the best random-forest fit at mtry = 15, the first minimum OOB classification error was 0.489 at 8 trees, and the final random-forest model was refit using that tree count.

Random Forest Feature Importance

Rank the final random-forest miRNA candidates by holdout-model feature importance and retain the top M miRNA as the final RF miRNA list.

The final random-forest miRNA list keeps the top 25 miRNA ranked by MeanDecreaseAccuracy from the refit forest using mtry = 15 and ntree = 8.

Decision Tree Cross-Validated Classification Error

We use a minimizing function to select the optimal tree size, which is 4.

Lasso and Elastic Net Optimal Alpha

We use GridSearchCV to find the optimal alpha for both Lasso and Elastic Net models.

## The optimal alpha for the Lasso model is 0.450000.

Decision Tree Feature Importance

For the decision tree, we define selected miRNA as miRNA that appear in one or more primary splits of the final fitted tree, and we separately report the tree’s variable-importance ranking.

For the decision tree, miRNA were considered selected only if they appeared in one or more primary splits of the final tree. The ranking below summarizes each miRNA’s total loss reduction across the fitted tree and distinguishes miRNA used in primary splits from miRNA with nonzero importance only.

Decision-tree miRNA feature-importance ranking on the final holdout model

Downstream multiMiR analysis uses all 9 miRNAs in this non-tree consensus set.

Multiple miRNAs tied for the highest non-tree model-selection count, so the table below is ordered by the smallest blocked-limma p-value, with ties broken by the absolute moderated t-statistic and then miRNA identifier. All rows in this table are used for downstream multiMiR annotation.

Multiple variables tied for the highest non-tree model-selection count, so miRNA variables in the table below are ordered by the smallest blocked-limma p-value, with ties broken by the absolute moderated t-statistic and then variable identifier. Demographic covariates do not have blocked-limma statistics and are listed after miRNA variables. The first row is the top-ranked consensus variable used for downstream annotation.

Comparison of Top-Ranked miRNA by Decision Tree and Random Forest

Limited to top 20 miRNAs for visualization.

decision_tree_top_miRNA <- final_model_outputs[["decision_tree"]]$selected_miRNA
random_forest_top_miRNA <- final_model_outputs[["random_forest"]]$selected_miRNA
decision_tree_top_miRNA <- head(decision_tree_top_miRNA, 20)
random_forest_top_miRNA <- head(random_forest_top_miRNA, 20)

comparison_df <- tibble(
  miRNA = union(decision_tree_top_miRNA, random_forest_top_miRNA),
  Decision_Tree = miRNA %in% decision_tree_top_miRNA,
  Random_Forest = miRNA %in% random_forest_top_miRNA
)

comparison_df_long <- comparison_df %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c("Decision_Tree", "Random_Forest"), names_to = "Model", values_to = "Selected")

ggplot(comparison_df_long, aes(x = Model, y = miRNA, fill = Selected)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "steelblue", "FALSE" = "grey90")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Comparison of Top-Ranked miRNA by Decision Tree and Random Forest", x = "Model", y = "miRNA")

Merging of Selected and Ranked Features by All Models

We will take the top 5 consensus miRNA features from the non tree-based models and merge them with the top 5 features from the decision tree and random forest models to create a comprehensive set of top-ranked features for further analysis.
